perturbation.original_text: If either party materially breaches one or more of its obligations hereunder, the other party may terminate this Agreement, effective upon written notice, if the breaching party does not cure such breach within 15 days after written notice thereof (or any mutually agreed extension).
perturbation.changed_text: If either party materially breaches one or more of its obligations hereunder, the other party may terminate this Agreement, effective upon written notice, if the breaching party does not cure such breach within 5 days after written notice thereof (or any mutually agreed extension).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from 15 days to 5 days may be considered unreasonably short and contradict the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing present in New York contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract.
perturbation.law_citation: New York Uniform Commercial Code, Section 1-304
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law requires that all parties act in good faith. A 5 day cure period may be insufficient time to reasonably cure the breach.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the cure period for a material breach from 15 days to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation states this may be unreasonably short and contradict the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing present in New York contracts. The Law Explanation states that New York law requires parties to act in good faith, and a 5-day cure period may be insufficient. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC 1-304, reinforces that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Reducing the cure period to 5 days could be seen as violating this obligation of good faith, thus creating a legal contradiction because the changed text sets a deadline that violates state law regarding good faith.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues in perpetuity, unless termination occurs pursuant to Sections 4.2 through 4.4.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues in perpetuity, unless termination occurs pursuant to Sections 4.2 through 4.4. Any action relating to a breach of this agreement must commence within six months of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a six-month limitation period for breach of contract claims conflicts with New York's six-year statute of limitations for such claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York statute of limitations for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_citation: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 213
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR § 213 provides a six-year statute of limitations for contract actions. Reducing this to six months is against the state law.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 2 - Limitations of Time 213 - Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract; on Sealed Instrument; on Bond or Note, and Mortgage Upon Real Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 213 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property; based on mistake; by corporation against director, officer or stockholder; based on fraud. The following actions must be commenced within six years: 1. an action for which no limitation is specifically prescribed by law; 2. an action upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied, except as provided in section two hundred thirteen-a or two hundred fourteen-i of this article or article 2 of the uniform commercial code or article 36-B of the general business law; 3. an action upon a sealed instrument; 4. an action upon a bond or note, the payment of which is secured by a mortgage upon real property, or upon a bond or note and mortgage so secured, or upon a mortgage of real property, or any interest therein; (a) In any action on an instrument described under this subdivision, if the statute of limitations is raised as a defense, and if that defense is based on a claim that the instrument at issue was accelerated
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a six-month limitation period for breach of contract claims, which directly contradicts the six-year statute of limitations provided by New York CPLR § 213 for contract actions. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states that actions upon a contractual obligation or liability must be commenced within six years, except as otherwise provided. The changed text attempts to provide an exception, but this exception is not legally valid, thereby creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, in the event that Licensee or any sublicensee is deemed to own any rights in the Brand, Licensee hereby irrevocably assigns (or shall cause such sublicensees to assign), without further consideration, such rights to Licensor together with all goodwill associated therewith.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, in the event that Licensee or any sublicensee is deemed to own any rights in the Brand, Licensee hereby irrevocably assigns (or shall cause such sublicensees to assign), without further consideration, such rights to Licensor together with all goodwill associated therewith. Any dispute relating to this agreement must be resolved through binding arbitration within the state of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement stipulates that it is to be governed by New York law, per Section 9.4. Forcing arbitration in Delaware contradicts that, because it could be seen as an attempt to circumvent the chosen law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Because the agreement states it is governed by New York law, imposing arbitration in Delaware may be seen as an attempt to circumvent New York law and thus may be invalid.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces binding arbitration in Delaware, while the original agreement is governed by New York law. This creates an inconsistency because it could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the chosen law, potentially undermining the enforceability of the agreement under New York law. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this potential contradiction. Although the provided scraped snippets primarily address the choice of law generally, the core issue is whether forcing arbitration in a different jurisdiction undermines the chosen law (New York). The added arbitration clause introduces a legal risk that did not exist in the original agreement, which is why there is a strong contradiction.
